a: Task mix varies materially with project complexity, installation scope, CNC maturity, and subcontracting.

rho: BLS evidence on CNC adoption does not establish AI implementation or benefits in bespoke small shops.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Custom Architectural Woodwork and Millwork Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: B2B project revenue through fixed-price, milestone, change-order, or time-and-material jobs, often supported by repeat architect, contractor, owner, retail, hospitality, workplace, and institutional programs; economics depend on takeoffs, submittals, shop drawings, procurement, fabrication, finishing, delivery, and installation coordination.
lens.excluded_activities: Factory-made commodity millwork, stock office or store fixtures, cabinets classified elsewhere, installers without manufacturing, captive internal shops, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market job-shop manufacturers of custom architectural woodwork, millwork, interiors, wall panels, display fixtures, counters, shelving, entrance or window details, and matching furniture supplied to external architects, general contractors, retailers, hotels, offices, institutions, and repeat program customers.

a.bridge: BLS describes woodworking duties and automation across occupations rather than wage-weighted AI exposure in 337212. I map architectural and shop-drawing review, takeoffs, estimating, submittals, procurement, scheduling, change orders, production documentation, quality records, customer coordination, and administration into exposed wages, while treating machining, finishing, assembly, packing, delivery, and installation as predominantly physical.
caveats: Occupation evidence spans woodworking industries and is not specific to architectural millwork.
Task mix varies materially with project complexity, installation scope, CNC maturity, and subcontracting.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from 10-15 eligible firms, with time studies for estimating, takeoffs, CAD or BIM, submittals, procurement, project management, CNC programming, machining, finishing, assembly, installation coordination, QA, and administration.
a.rationale: Custom millwork is information-heavy before fabrication: each job requires interpretation of drawings and specifications, material and hardware selection, detailed shop drawings, bids, approvals, schedules, and coordination. Census also characterizes output as individually ordered job-shop work requiring skilled craftspeople, limiting exposure in the production layer.

caveats: Construction spending does not isolate millwork budgets or distinguish renovation from new build.
Broader furniture output is not a six-digit architectural-millwork demand series.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price architectural woodwork shipments, Architectural Woodwork Institute member backlogs, project starts and renovation forecasts by end market, contractor bid pipelines, cancellation rates, import penetration, modular substitution, and customer capex plans.
d5.rationale: Renovation, hospitality, retail, institutional, workplace, and premium residential fit-outs support ongoing demand, while repeat programs and custom requirements limit direct commoditization. Construction cyclicality, office weakness, delayed projects, prefabricated systems, and broad furniture-output declines create downside; the May 2026 construction data are context rather than a direct millwork forecast.

caveats: The 2,371 employer-establishment count does not reveal ownership, firm consolidation, or investment eligibility.
Establishments may overstate firms and include businesses with materially different project and installation models.
e.change_evidence: An establishment-to-owner map followed by verification of domestic value-add, external and repeat revenue, customer concentration, normalized EBITDA, backlog quality, bonding, change-order discipline, owner dependency, equipment, installation exposure, and project-level margins.
e.rationale: The job-shop definition and broad establishment base imply many external suppliers with repeat contractor, architect, and owner relationships. Eligibility is reduced by captive shops, installation-only businesses, owner-centric estimating, poor backlog quality, bonding constraints, single-contractor dependence, weak controls, chronic fixed-price overruns, and firms outside normalized LMM control economics.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 337212 ($1,847,060,598) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 337212 ($9,377,672,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $215,757 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 337212); class <5: 860 firms, avg revenue/firm $382,484 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $75,043 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 437 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,156,970 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $226,998 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 387 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,483,411 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $487,245 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 516 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,291,936 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,626,878 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 76 firms, avg revenue/firm $31,365,237 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,153,859 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $36,786,520 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,217,515 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 617

caveats: Operator requirement is inferred from the physical, custom product definition.
Modular substitution and project cancellation belong primarily in d5 and are not double-counted here.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on contractor or owner insourcing, modular and digitally fabricated substitutes, autonomous factories, design-to-fabrication platforms, imports, and manufacturer versus installer responsibility in completed projects.
o.rationale: The current basket requires an accountable operator to engineer, fabricate, finish, quality-check, deliver, and often coordinate installation of bespoke physical interiors. Software can improve design and procurement but cannot supply the finished product; customer self-fabrication and fully autonomous local production remain limited.

caveats: No public contract sample quantifies pass-through.
Retention is likely higher for complex specified interiors than for standardized fixtures or price-led bid packages.
q.change_evidence: Bid tabs, customer and GC agreements, open-book clauses, award rates, change-order recoveries, repeat-customer retention, backlog conversion, lead-time premiums, and project gross margins before and after workflow automation.
q.rationale: Design coordination, drawing accuracy, trusted execution, finish quality, schedule performance, field problem-solving, and scarce skilled capacity can retain meaningful benefits through faster bids, lower rework, and better throughput. General-contractor bids, owner procurement, competitive rebids, open-book contracts, and value engineering transfer part of visible savings to customers.

caveats: No direct five-year adoption study for eligible firms was found.
BLS evidence on CNC adoption does not establish AI implementation or benefits in bespoke small shops.
rho.change_evidence: Pre/post deployments for drawing interpretation, takeoffs, estimating, shop-drawing support, procurement, scheduling, CNC programming, visual QA, change orders, and administration, measuring cycle time, rework, errors, award rates, gross margin, labor hours, and on-time completion.
rho.rationale: AI-assisted drawing and specification review, takeoffs, estimating, submittals, procurement, scheduling, change-order support, and documentation are feasible within five years, and CNC already demonstrates digital production potential. Realization is constrained by bespoke details, incomplete drawings, fragmented historical job data, legacy systems, field conditions, approvals, physical inspection, and small-firm integration capacity.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sell or transfer intentions among US employer-business owners across industries, not completed control sales in architectural millwork. I center near the reported 22 percent intention and modestly raise the base for mature owner-operated craft manufacturers, while discounting internal transfers, closures, asset sales, distress, and transactions that do not complete.
caveats: Cross-industry intentions are not completed industry transactions.
Internal family transfers, closures, and distressed asset sales may not qualify as control opportunities.
s5.change_evidence: Closed architectural millwork transactions from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, specialty advisers, and brokers matched to a verified eligible denominator, plus owner-age, succession-plan, customer-concentration, and key-person data.
s5.rationale: Equipment, craftspeople, backlog, contractor relationships, drawings, and estimating history can transfer, but owner relationships, key estimators, project risk, bonding, and concentration can impair a qualifying sale.

principal_driver: An unusually information-heavy estimating, shop-drawing, submittal, procurement, and project-coordination layer sits ahead of skilled custom production.
principal_weakness: Cyclical project demand and owner- or estimator-dependent fixed-price execution can erase workflow gains through overruns and weak backlog quality.
